questions.title: What happens if a state variable used inside a useEffect hook is omitted from its dependency array?
options: React will throw a critical compile-time error and halt execution.
The effect closure captures the initial value of the state and fails to see subsequent updates (stale state).
The effect automatically detects the variable and re-runs anyway to prevent errors.
The state variable is swept from browser memory to prevent leaks.

questions.title: Why should you never directly mutate a state array or prop array using methods like .sort() or .push() in React?
options: Because JavaScript arrays are structurally immutable by default in modern runtimes.
It mutates the underlying reference in place, bypassing React's reconciliation engine and preventing re-renders.
It forces the child components to automatically switch into read-only mode.

questions.title: If two completely separate components consume the exact same custom React hook, how is their state managed?
options: They share a single synchronized state instance; updating it in one updates it in both.
Each component instance instantiates its own isolated, completely independent copy of the hook's state.
The second component to mount overrides and locks out the state of the first component.

questions.title: In React's <StrictMode>, why do components execute their render phase twice during development?
options: To verify that the local dev server's hot-module-reloading mechanism is active.
To intentionally surface unintended side effects, helping ensure components behave as pure functions.
To pre-render both the standard view and the fallback layout simultaneously.

questions.title: Why is using an array element's loop index as its 'key' prop discouraged when list items can be re-ordered, filtered, or deleted?
options: It forces the browser to re-download the layout engine from the network.
Indices are primitive numbers and React strictly requires cryptographic strings for keys.
React maps internal state to the index; if the data shifts but the index remains, the wrong state maps to the wrong UI element.

grades.message: Absolute React Deity. Dan Abramov is shaking. You completely mastered the nuance of reconciliation and closures!
Solid Production-Ready Engineer. You've got a fantastic grasp on hooks and the component lifecycle.
Junior Dev Energy. You've got the basics down, but watch out for those state mutation and rendering traps!
Back to the docs! Time to write some more custom markdown documentation files to iron out the fundamentals.
